title: Minimize the admission of containers with allowPrivilegeEscalation

input_row.title: Minimize the admission of containers with allowPrivilegeEscalation
input_row.description: Do not generally permit containers to be run with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true. Allowing this right can lead to a process running a container getting more rights than it started with. It's important to note that these rights are still constrained by the overall container sandbox, and this setting does not relate to the use of privileged containers.
input_row.rationale: A container running with the allowPrivilegeEscalation flag set to true may have processes that can gain more privileges than their parent. There should be at least one admission control policy defined which does not permit containers to allow privilege escalation. The option exists (and is defaulted to true) to permit setuid binaries to run. If you have need to run containers which use setuid binaries or require privilege escalation, this should be defined in a separate policy and you should carefully check to ensure that only limited service accounts and users are given permission to use that policy.
input_row.audit: List the policies in use for each namespace in the cluster, ensure that each policy disallows the admission of containers which allow privilege escalation. To fetch a list of pods which allowPrivilegeEscalation run this command :- get pods -A -o=jsonpath=$'{range .items[*]}{@.metadata.name}: {@..securityContext}\n{end}'
input_row.remediation: Add policies to each namespace in the cluster which has user workloads to restrict the admission of containers with securityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: true Internal Only - General
input_row.impact: Pods defined with securityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: true will not be permitted unless they are run under a specific policy.
input_row.default_value: By default, there are no restrictions on contained process ability to escalate privileges, within the context of the container.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit mentions using `kubectl` to list pods and check their security context for the `allowPrivilegeEscalation` flag.
- This property can be queried via Kubernetes APIs and `kubectl`, making it suitable for an automated approach.

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_pod_security_allow_privilege_escalation_restricted

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the `kubectl get pods -A -o=jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{@.metadata.name}: {@..securityContext.allowPrivilegeEscalation}\\n{end}'` command to retrieve the list of all pods across all namespaces and their `allowPrivilegeEscalation` settings.
- Check the output for any instances where `allowPrivilegeEscalation` is set to `true`.
- Pass/Fail criteria:
  - **Pass:** No pods have `allowPrivilegeEscalation` set to `true`.
  - **Fail:** At least one pod has `allowPrivilegeEscalation` set to `true`.
- Additionally, ensure that there are admission control policies in place that restrict the admission of containers with `allowPrivilegeEscalation: true`. This can be verified by checking the configuration of the admission controllers in the API server settings.
